front: Building: rebuilt after the Great Fire of 1666, this London cathedral on Ludgate Hill contains a Whispering Gallery below its dome.
Building: Wren's tomb inside this cathedral reads 'Reader, if you seek his monument, look around you.'
Building: this cathedral's dome was inspired by Mansart's Val-de-Grace and the Romanesque lantern at Ely, where the architect's uncle was bishop.
Building: this Wren cathedral features woodwork by Grinling Gibbons and ironwork by Jean Tijou.
Building: Herbert Mason's 1940 photograph of this cathedral — dome and two spires visible above Blitz smoke — became an iconic image of wartime resilience.
Building: Bill Viola's Martyrs was commissioned for this cathedral, which also houses William Holman Hunt's The Light of the World.
Building: this cathedral has a three-shelled double dome with a Stone Gallery and Golden Gallery, flanked by two spires, with a facade of paired Corinthian columns.
Building: the eastern limewood paneling of this Wren cathedral lists the names of 28,000 American soldiers who died in Britain during WWII.
Building: the tomb of John Donne is inside this London cathedral, alongside that of its architect.
Building: this Wren-designed 202-foot fluted Doric column has an interior staircase leading to a golden urn of fire at its top.
back: The Monument to the Great Fire of London (Christopher Wren)
work: The Monument to the Great Fire of London

front: Building: this Wren-designed London column was bombed by Martial Bourdin in what was described as the first international terrorist attack.
back: The Monument to the Great Fire of London (Christopher Wren)
work: The Monument to the Great Fire of London

front: Building: John Flamsteed was the first resident of this Wren-designed Greenwich building, built to house his astronomical work.
Building: tall windows were installed in the Octagon Room of this Wren-designed Greenwich observatory for astronomical observation.
Building: this Wren-designed observatory in Greenwich marks the prime meridian.
Building: Wren designed the library of this Cambridge college.
Building: William and Mary tasked Wren to remodel this palace to rival Versailles, but the project was abandoned in 1694, leaving a mix of Baroque and Tudor architecture.
Building: Wren designed this Greenwich building that now serves as a naval college.
Building: Wren designed the oldest collegiate building in the United States, located at the College of William and Mary.
back: Wren Building, College of William and Mary (Christopher Wren)

front: Building: a false ceiling in this cathedral conceals beams Wren installed to support its tower.
Building: Wren designed this Oxford theatre, though credit for some of his works is now given to his office, including Nicholas Hawksmoor.
Architect: before turning to architecture, this man created illustrations for Thomas Willis's Cerebri Anatome — the book that introduced the word 'neurology.'
Architect: this English Baroque architect took Nicholas Hawksmoor as an apprentice.
Architect: this architect worked with Robert Hooke to rebuild London after the Great Fire of 1666.
Architect: as MP for Weymouth, this architect controlled nearby Portland stone quarries he used for St. Paul's Cathedral.
Architect: John Dryden's poem Annus Mirabilis commemorates this architect's works rebuilding London after the Great Fire.
Architect: Charles Demuth's watercolor After Sir Christopher Wren depicts the Provincetown Heritage Museum, whose design was inspired by this architect.
Architect: Peter Ackroyd's novel Hawksmoor follows a detective solving murders at churches designed by a Satanist who worked alongside this English Baroque architect.
Architect: this architect lamented 'ladies think nothing well without an edging.'
